sentences.text: So life-- I was just explaining before, life in the camp, even the worst, you know, hell, like when you describe hell, you know, Dante Alighieri, one, you know, wrote that there about you know, purgatory-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --and hell and heaven, but never as imagine what hell, that it was worse than hell.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
And I don't know how, but myself, personally, I never thought I was going to die in the concentration camp.
I always say, I will live.
I will live.
And I will tell the world all the things happened, the evil things.
The world should know.
That, it was my aim, you know, my survival.
And then even if I'm the last person to survive, I will not die.
Because I had that willing.
Because certain amount of the people who survive also, they had a little bit of will to live.
But if you let yourself, you know, discourage and be upset, and then that was the end.
Because you were so thin.
Normally, you know, one person, you know, there, even that I was very thin in Rhodes Island.
When I left, you know, I was young girl, that I had, for instance, like a few kilos.
There, I was really, you know, like-- something like 60 pounds.
A-- a grown-up woman, you know, 60 pounds is very, very little.
And then you can see your bones through-- through your chest, and whatever it is.
But-- and you cannot even-- the strength, you know, to walk.
But funny enough, when I was in the concentration camp, I was very, very strong morally and physically.
I never felt hunger.
Because I let myself courage, courage.
And-- and I was trying, you know, to do whatever in my power, you know, to-- to survive.
Once I was telling you about my sister.
She was very thin, which usually she was plump during all the-- usual time.
Eventually, one day she said, listen, I've got enough.
I can't live anymore.
And I'm feeling very sick in they way that I've got this sore-- I've got a earache.
Must be an infection.
I want to go to hospital.
I beg her not to go.
But she say, I can't.
Listen, I will die all the same you know, in the-- in the counting or the appell, whatever it is.
That is in the 40 days that we had the quarantine time.
She said, I'll go to the hospital.
Whatever happen, happen.
I heard that in the hospital, from other prisoner, in the hospital, they give you a pill when you go to sleep.
And in the morning they find you dead.
It was poison.
I say, all right.
You want to go, you rest.
But don't take any pills.
Whatever medicine they will give you, they will take-- you will take there not any pills.
She promised me.
She promise me not to take any pills.
Eventually, you know, she-- that night, she went.
And they give her the pill that she told me afterwards.
She didn't took it.
And she took it, she put it under the-- she put it under the pillow.
It was the-- not the pillow, under the straw.
There was no pillows or-- or mattresses or anything.
She put it under the straw in the hospital.
She was lucky.
You know, the hospital, there was straw in the-- in the-- in the planks.
But where we stayed, there was no-- no-- no straw in it.
It was just planks.
I'll tell you just now, what that our bed consist of now.
So my sister, she didn't took the-- the pill that they give it to her.
And she hide it in the straw.
The next morning, the nurses, they were very surprised to find her alive.
Say, aren't you dead?
Say, no.
I'm all right.
And then they were very surprised.
They were waiting for the next night, to give her another pill.
So now, that-- that same night, I wanted to visit her and see how she was doing.
As it was, I told you, these blocks, they were made out of wood.
So I took my portion of the bread.
Whenever you had bread in the camp, it's like having millions in the-- in the bank.
You know, you can do anything you want with the bread.
I took the portion of the bread.
I didn't eat it.
And I was going to see what I can do with this bread to see my sister.
So first of all, I went around the block-- it was made in wood-- knocking at every single corner.
My sister's name is Diamonte.
And I would say, Diamonte, are you here?
Diamonte, are you here?
Until she say, yes, I am here.
So I look around.
And I see exactly the-- the location, where she was sleeping.
So I knew it was, like you say, for instance, the seven or eight bed.
So I went to the entrance.
So beside the-- the nurses and all the other were entering that hospital, they were the prisoner like us.

But they were giving a white handkerchief to put around their head to be able to enter there, to bring like the food, and to bring like, the dirty out of the hospital, like, dirty tissues, dirty whatever, blood, or whatever.
So though I didn't understand them, I didn't speak, you know, German.
I saw a girl.
I don't know what language she was.
I show her the bread, if she can give me, she can just lend me her handkerchief for half an hour.
It was the bread, it was my survival of one day.
But it doesn't matter.
And then she agreed.
Because as I told you, bread was the best money in the world.
If you had millions in the American dollars, you had millions in jewelry, it doesn't count.
What it counts there?
Bread.
Bread, it was life or death, you know, in camp.
It counts.
So the girl agree.
And she went to, you know, hiding.
And while, you know, some other, you know, helping the other girl with the barrel of dirty to be taken in or whatever, taken out, I took the handkerchief.
And I went with the girl inside of the hospital.
Because no prisoner was allowed into the hospital.
Only the sick people and-- and the nurses.
But no other prisoner were allowed.
So I went with this barrel of-- of food, or whatever it was.
And I went.
And I knew exactly where my sister were.
While the girl deposited the barrel in the floor, I run quick.
And I went into her direction.
And I went to sleep with her.
And I covered myself behind her.
Nobody saw me, that I was sitting there.
And I stay for-- for one hour or so talking to her, and begging her to-- to come back to the-- to the block.
And say, how can I do?
Say, whatever you can do, you come out.
And don't take the pill.
Next to her, you know, in another bed, was another friend of mine.
Her name-- her name was [?
Sylvia ?] [INAUDIBLE].
She was a very friend of mine.
And she had come to the-- to the hospital because she had a little bit of rheumatism.
Otherwise, she was a very strong girl.
But that night, they give her the pill, and she died during the night.
So the next morning, my-- my sister promise me to come back, you know, while they were-- the-- the nurses were, you know, busy, to come back into the block 20.
So which she did the next day.
And she came to the block 20, while we were in the count.
So now these girls the block over start counting and counting.
There was one extra-- extra prisoner.
Say, where she come from?
They're counting again.
Nobody will tell who-- nobody knew.
Because they were doing the counting.
They were counting five, five, five.
There is one extra prisoner.
They know when one prisoner goes to hospital, she'll never be coming back.
Because they-- she has been eliminated for sure.
She doesn't count anymore.
Her name was already eradicated from-- from the list.
She will never be alive again.
As I told you, they give-- will give the-- the pill.
And then eventually, you know, they-- they-- they-- they-- they-- they were making the numbers, you know, the appell.
They find it was her.
It were-- and they found it was her.
And they took it from one place of the-- you know, from the ear.
They pick her from the ear.
And threw her in the floor.
And she knock her head, and she was nearly dead.
Eventually-- that's [? at ?] [? that ?] very moment, the doctors arrive with the appell again.
You know?
They want to make a-- you know, a-- a selection again, for people to be taken to the hospital.
And-- and then my sister, who was in the-- with the confusion, she was again in the-- in the block, you know, in the block where we were.
Because they were taking so many away, you know, in the confusion.
So many taken away to be-- to be-- to be-- to be dead, you know, to be killed, that they didn't count it anymore, one extra, one after.
One day while we was walking, a friend of ours, you know, in our group, she found, you know, a human bone, lying dead in the-- in the snow.
And we were all so excited.
She took that human bone, and she start sucking.
There was nothing to suck in it.
But just, you know, something to put into your mouth.
We was very, very hungry.
There was no grass.
There was nothing you can put in your mouth.
And then everybody begging, let me suck.
Let me suck.
And we knew exactly, it belongs to a human leg.
But we were just sucking all the same, you know?
Sucking that bone.
And finding a little bit comfort, that sucking something right now.
Because we were really very hungry.
Sometimes you stand there.
And you fall down because from starvation.
You know?
From starvation.
No water, no food in our-- in our-- in our-- in your body.
Eventually, you know, we went doing those things for 40 days in the quarantine, that it was, that day, the quarantine.
And at the end of it, my-- and you know, everybody, us left the camp.
All our group have left the camp.
And only us were remaining in Auschwitz.
And then from one selection to another selection, I don't know.
Because many people, they don't-- they think it's fate.
It's not fate.
To-- to me, according to me, it was God's will that we can be alive.
Because it's impossible, so many selection we went through.
The last one, it was the worst of the worst.
Nobody will come alive and be selection.
It was, you know, the-- I mean, maybe I jump the times, whatever it is.
But it comes, you know, 25th of January.
And it was the last selection of the Auschwitz camp.
And they came, you know?
And they were selecting to take to Germany, to work.
And we were selected.
We were sorted.
We were selected to be killed that same night.
Previously to that same night, they have come, you know, like, three, days before.
They have selected, you know, another group.
Amongst there, there was my little sister, you know?
And they were counting and counting.
And they stop at the previous selection.
And then she was, you know, she has to be killed also in the crematorium.
The next selection, the next day, it was our turn to be selected again, to be killed.
We didn't see her anymore, my little sister.
Before you go on, now to continue, can you just tell me, were people still observing the religious ideas in the camp?
Ah.
I mean, now and then yes, we were just-- Shabbat came.
You know, every Shabbat, when we went to bed, there was, you know, a young girl, you know, was my friend.
She was sleeping next to myself and my sister.
She was very observant in Rhodes Island.
She was very religious.
Every night, after, you know, we went to bed on Shabbat, she would sing all the same songs from the synagogue.
And she will, you know, keep us like-- like going on.
And then, you know, it came October.
When we arrive, it was August.
It came October, and it was Yom Kippur.
That year it fell in 1944, it fells in Yom Kippur, on October-- in October.
And then, you know, of course we say, today's Kippur, we won't eat.
And then, we ask-- we ask the [GERMAN] to-- you know, to intervene to the Germans.
If the food-- our food of that night of Kippur, could be given to us the next-- the next day.
And then they say, yes, they will give it to us.
Come the next day, we're all, you know, fasting, it was Kippur.
The next day came, they're giving us nothing.
Because say, you didn't want to eat.
And it's Kippur.
We didn't eat that-- that night.
And-- you know, beside that, you know, the-- when it was, you know, any other festival, we knew.
I don't know how I knew.
We had no calendar.
We had nothing.
We know when it was Rosh Hashanah.
We knew everything.
Though we were working, but we were trying to-- to remember, how it was, the good times, when we were in Rhodes Island. how it was, the shul, how it was.
We were talking each other.
Otherwise, we were just, you know, lucky enough to survive.
And we didn't know.
We were like beasts, I can tell you the truth.
We were not, you know, like-- we had no feelings for nothing.
We had no feelings for nothing, you see?
And we were just [? hurting. ?] And I mean, tell you about the-- the beds, how horrible they were.
They were, you know, four-- four planks.
Four planks with no nails or nothing, going like, four nails, how you put it in the table.
And the four nails, you know, they had three layers of planks.
One layers, the plank's going the-- you know? and-- and that-- in that piece of wood, they were laying some planks like what, you know, from here.
Like you say, call it two meters.
On top, there was another layers, the same thing.
And then top-- at third floor.
The-- the-- in each one-- in each one layer there, we have to sleep, 12 people.
But even if you put, you know, one next to the other one, you will never fit in two meters three-- 12 bodies.
So it means to say, we were all-- we were without even blankets.
We were all in the same direction.
If during the night, you know, you are tired of sleeping in one side, and we want turn to the other side, they would say, girls, can we turn?
All of the 12 people would turn the other side.
Because if you take the one person sleeping one direction, and one into the other side, we will never fit.
So much-- so much we were moving and making noise and everything.
So they hoped that it was just, you know, to make us, you know, demoralized.
The whole thing will collapse.
Because there were no nails.
So the 14 people in there first, the second, and the third, we found ourself all on the floor with the planks around us.
So we will start all over again, making our bed, putting the four planks together.
This happen every night, two, three times.
So it means to say, we weren't sleeping, and screaming and fighting each other, until you know, we start having a little bit of rest.
Come four o'clock, we have to go out in the snow.
So we never sleep either.
We never sleep in the day.
We never sleep in the night.
Some people, you know, like, they were shift at night, you know?
And some, they were shift at days in the factories.
You know, while we where there, they had already done their quarantine.
They will never dare sleep either, even they had to shift, you know, at night, and they could sleep during the day.
They will never dare sleep at the day.
Because it was the moment of the distribution of the food.
If they sleep, you know, during the distribution, they would never ate food.
So they-- all of us, we never slept, day or night.
They were, you know, in a mass of humanity, suffering, humanity, unbelievable.
What, you know, we suffered there.
So I was saying today-- Before you continue, what was your relationship like with the other prisoners, with the other Jewish people?
Were there non-Jewish people in the camp?
No, because we knew-- the Jewish people were kept in one-- in one block, in one-- you know, in one thing.
And there were other ones, political.
There were political people, like there were non-Jewish, could be, you know, Germans, Russians, whatever.
They were political.
They were separated in another-- I don't know how their condition.
I think there was a little bit-- the political were treated a little bit better than us.
And then, you know, there were the Gypsies.
The Gypsies also in another-- in another block, that they were different.
I mean, another-- what to distinguish, you know, them from us?
It was-- they had-- in there-- you know, they wear a-- a triangle.
The Jewish people, they had the yellow triangle, and we were Jewish.
The political, they had the white.
And the-- and the Gypsy, they had another kind of the distinguishment, you know, that they were not-- they were Gypsies.
So we, in our block, they were all Jewish.
But from all over Europe.
There were Polish people, German people, [? all ?] [? land ?] people.
And we find the most, you know, friendly people, the most human people, like you say, the Hungarian.
The Hungarian, you know, girls, they were very, very good.
They were trying, you know, to-- to help us.
Because they knew German.
And but they didn't know, you know, but they were very good girls.
But some other girls, they were also cruel to us.
Because we're from, you know, Spanish descendant, we don't speak-- we don't speak Yiddish.
And according to them, we were not Jewish.
They say, are you Jewish?
I say, of course we are Jewish, late-- later on, you know, we start to learn a little bit of German, to communicate.
Say, are you Jewish?
And then we say, yes, we are Jewish.
Say, no, you speak-- you speak Yiddish?
We say, no.
We don't.
You're not Jewish.
And then but you say, listen, the-- the Torah is not written in Yiddish.
It's written in Hebrew.
We can speak Hebrew.
Because all of us, we went to Jewish school.
We can speak Hebrew.
But I mean, it was little bit of you know, it was [? understanding ?] and this and that.
But at the end, we were all suffering the same fate.
And were all-- we were calling each other sister of-- of-- like you say, [ITALIAN].
In Italian, it is [ITALIAN], like you say, bad luck.
Sister of bad luck, we used to call, you know, each other.
Everybody what-- even after the camp, we meet them in Italy.
We were all called sisters.
You know?
We were all sisters.
Did you see your father again after that one time that you mentioned?
Well, no.
I-- in the-- well, during the quarantine time, I been telling you, 40 days, the men were separated by the woman by this barbed wire, you know, electric barbed wires.
But we can see them every day for 40 days.
That's why, you know, he was telling us all the advice.
And many times, you know, when you were-- and then many times though we were three sisters and we needed that bread, I know my-- you know, my brothers, two brothers and my father were men, you know?
They were used to eat more than we-- than the girl.
Sometimes we were trying, you know, to save a piece of our-- of our ration.
And we were throwing over-- over the ones for them, to have them extra.
They were having the same amount of, you know, piece of bread.
But we thought we can sacrifice ourselves.
We were girls.
We were not so-- I mean, we have good appetite, like, in normal time.
But the-- the boys, my brothers, they were 22, 24, just in the prime of their life.
And they used to have the best thing in Rhodes Island, you know?
And then we were trying-- we were trying to help-- we were trying to help them, you know, to have a little bit, extra food.
And another thing, you know, my sister, beside that time she went to hospital, she was there-- she was taken by the-- the-- we used to call the malady of the camp.
It means the-- the-- the sickness of the camp.
That is as much hungry as you are aware all the time.
When the sickness of the camp grabs you, it was like a disease, you know?
Then you have lost your appetite.
You don't want to eat anything else, even if they give you the best food in the world.
It's like anorexia.
You know?
When you drink, you know, when you a certain-- when you can't eat anymore, the same thing happened in the camp.
The certain, you know, time when you were so much sick, you're couldn't eat anymore.
And then you stay one day or two day without food.
Eventually you die.
Because that it was the fate.
So everybody was trying to eat, even you know, when they were sick, and to push the other friends that they were sick, of this sickness, not to abandon hope and to abandon, you know, the food.
So one or two times, my sister was-- was caught, you know, by this disease.
And she didn't want to eat, my older sister.
So myself also, with my little sister, she was there, we had two-- the three ration.
My sister wouldn't eat her ration.
I took her ration of bread.
And I went to the kitchen and-- and there were those girls working there.
And I give the ration of one-- of one-- of day if she can give me one clove of garlic.
Because this garlic, you know, in Rhodes Island, we used to use so much in our cuisine.
And like they say, they makes you build your appetite.
So we used to take that garlic, you know, that clove of garlic and you know, put it into the bread, like butter, like that to dissolve.
And it was giving a little flavor to the bread.
And then you're giving to my sister like medicine, and she will-- she will live.
Another time, you know, myself, you know, I had a-- a very big ball, which I've got still here the-- the-- the-- the scar.
My arm was very, very swollen, full of pus.
And then, you know, the only thing to do was to go to the hospital.
But I was very, very, very frightened to go to hospital.
I know it was going to be the end.

A friend of ours, you know, during the-- from Rhodes Island, she had that the husband that's given her-- and previously from another camp, while they were in quarantine they smuggled there a tiny-- tiny penknife, you know?
Over the barbed to-- to-- to cut the bread or whatever.
She had this hided.
Nobody knew where she had it hided, this-- this-- this penknife.
So we ask her if she can give it to us for a little while.
Because another friend, that the one she was very religious and keeping the Shabbat songs and all this, said, don't worry, I'll make the operation-- the operation on you.
And I say, OK.
And then, you know, she went into the-- into the snow, you know, outside it was snow.
She put the penknife, and it was, you know, like disinfectant, you know?
The very very cold, it kills the germs.
She came with this penknife, and she cutted my thing.
And if you see-- and it was painful.
But I have to-- to bear, you know, the pain better than going, you know, to be killed in the hospital.
Pus and pus and pus, the whole, you know, floor, and the whole, you know, thing of-- in the floor was covered in pus.
And then, you know, I squeeze and I squeeze and I squeeze.
And my friend also squeeze-- and we had no bandages.
We had nothing-- and until blood came out, you know?
Because like, real blood.
And then, I was so much in pain before the operation that I say all right, do it.
Whatever.
Penknife or no penknife, you know, make me better.
And then, you know, I-- I owe my life to that girl.
And then, you know, the next thing we'll do is, I took snow.
And I rub it against my-- my scar.
And like it was a miracle or whatever, it start getting better and better and better until only a little scar remain.
But I was better.
Because once the pus was out of my system, I was better again.
I don't know his-- know his-- his-- his designs, whatever it is.
But I believe in another, you know-- in the power of God.
Whatever happened to me, whatever happened, I think it was all by God.
The children?
There were no children.
As soon they arrive, they were put in to death with the old.
The old and the children, they would put in aside.
I know we see some films, they show that there were-- there were-- there were camps full of children.
But I didn't see any.
Because the children, there were some children that they want to make experiments with, to make-- to change the color of their eyes, to change their genes.
I don't know that.
But in my camp, I never saw a child.
Because as soon they arrive, they are-- they were put in to death.
As a matter of fact, when we were coming out of-- out of the-- of the wagons, and they were those Salonika, you know, Greek-speaking, Spanish-speaking people, they were advising.
We didn't know the meaning at the time.
They say, all the children, give it into the hands of the old.
All the children, we didn't know what it means.
It means, if a-- a mother was carrying a child, so it was certain death for the mother and the child.
Because they didn't want the mother you know, to leave without the child, or the child without the mother.
They were put into death, both.
But if a-- a woman was carrying an older-- an-- an old woman was carrying a baby, so these Salonika people thought, the old woman shall die anyhow.
So better not to carry an old person that a young baby that it will die anyhow.
And then, you know, the-- the life of the mother would be spared for the time being.
So we didn't have a single child.
I didn't see a single child.
No.
We didn't know a thing.
We didn't know.
Nobody will tell us.
Only-- the only thing we knew is that I don't know from what way it arrived to us, that the German were losing the war.
Some Germans girl, you know, that they speak German, maybe they were, you know, if it's-- you know, they were listening.
I don't know what it was.
They were giving us the hope.
And they said, don't worry.
It will be the soon, the-- the end.
But no-- nobody tried to escape or to evade or to [? then-- ?] because we can't.
You know, we couldn't.
There were, you know, these-- these Germans there in the towers, five or four of them, with their guns pointed to us daily and nightly.
And they wouldn't stop that.
So we couldn't do a thing.
And then all these wires, you know, they were electric, you know?
If you touch them, you will die instantly, hot-- high voltage.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] Did you ever stop believing in God--
No.
No.
That's what I say, never.
Never.
I say, God will take us out from here.
God will-- how the-- you know, he took us, the-- the Jews, out of Egypt, how he makes so many miracles.
